answer:
In the Urdu corpus (primarily *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, the three parts of Allama Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara*, and *Diwan-e-Zauq*), **'jigar'** (جِگَر) appears exclusively in metaphorical usage across dozens of ghazals and nazms, never literally as an organ. It functions as a poetic synecdoche for the core of human emotion—intense love (*ishq*), longing (*arzoo*), suffering (*dard*), sacrifice, and occasionally courage or resolve—often paired with *dil* (heart) as *dil-o-jigar* to evoke the body's deepest visceral turmoil. This aligns with classical Urdu/Persian poetic tradition, where the liver was poetically "boiled" (*gudaaz*) by passion or bled (*khoon-e-jigar*) like the heart.

Common motifs:
- **Khoon-e-jigar** (liver's blood): Lifeblood sacrificed to nurture hopeless love, ambitions, or faith.[^1][^2]
- **Zakhm/daagh-e-jigar** (liver wound/scar): Unhealable pain from separation or betrayal, too deep for remedy.[^3][^4]
- **Penetration to jigar** (e.g., gaze, arrow, or poison): Love's assault bypassing superficial *dil* to strike the soul's resilient core.[^5]
- **Jigar-gudaaz/goshta** (liver-melting/piece): Extreme agitation or fragmentation from grief.[^6]

Ghalib deploys it ~40+ times in *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, amplifying existential despair and ironic passion (e.g., *dil se teri nigaah jigar tak utar gayi*—"your glance descended from heart to liver").[^5] Iqbal uses it ~10 times in *Bang-e-Dara*, tying it to spiritual striving and communal lament (*Shikwa*), as in nourishing "garden" hopes with self-sacrifice.[^1] No occurrences in *Diwan-e-Zauq*.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 2 (“Anokhi waza hai...”), [p-61af92](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/02-anokhi-waza-hai-saare-zamane-se-nirale-hain/transliteration/#p-61af92). Transl: "I fed these shoots with my own heart's blood-rain." (*Jigar ka khoon...*)
[^2]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 40 (“Ek ek qatre ka...”), [p-9d6143](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/40-ek-ek-qatre-ka-mujhe-dena-pada-hisaab/transliteration/#p-9d6143).
[^3]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 134 (“Maze jahaan ke...”), [p-5d6c2c](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/134-maze-jahaan-ke-apni-nazar-mein-khaak-nahin/transliteration/#p-5d6c2c). (*Siwaaye khoon-e-jigar so jigar mein khaak nahin.*)
[^4]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 94 (“Jis zakhm ki...”), para. on *lab-e-zakhm-e-jigar*.
[^5]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 35 (“Dil se teri nigaah...”), [p-8219b3](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/35-dil-se-teri-nigaah-jigar-tak-utar-gayi/transliteration/#p-8219b3).
[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 17 (“Dard-e-Ishq”), [p-787d3f](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/transliteration/#p-787d3f). (*Ashk jigar gudaaz...*)
